18	
19	The
20	{at_rbm_system}
21	enables developers to safely run untrusted containers -
22	a significantly distinct use case than typical container deployments of carefully crafted code.
23	
24	While containers excel at packaging known applications,

33	without requiring modifications to existing container images.
34	
35	This approach allows developers to leverage the vast ecosystem of containerized tools while maintaining strict security boundaries -
36	essential for small teams experimenting with diverse, potentially risky codebases.
37	
38	== Accomplished Infrastructure
39	
40	The
41	{at_rbm_system}
42	migrated from GitHub Actions and GHCR to Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR) in August 2025.
43	GHCR violated OCI specification for reference counting, and GitHub Actions' shared runner environment enabled cache poisoning attacks — both unacceptable for building containers from untrusted code.
44	
45	GCB and GAR provide isolated build environments, SLSA Build Level 3 attestations (verified end-to-end March 2026), reference-counted layer management, and native vulnerability scanning.
46	The project's build infrastructure was converted from Makefiles to pure bash, and the container runtime switched from podman to docker in December 2025.
47	
48	== The {at_rbm_system} Vision
49	
50	.RBM System Architecture
51	image::rbm-abstract-drawio.svg[RBM Architecture,600,400,align="center"]
52	
53	Maintaining build and service environments is a headache for the small development organization.
54	{st_container_s}
55	are an amazing tool for controlling package version constellations.
56	Necessary, but insufficient; I find that I need more redundancy, security, and control for my build setups.
57	
58	The
59	{at_rbm_system}
60	is my answer for filling in the gaps.
61	The vision is simple: how can an
62	{at_operator}
63	run only a few apps natively on a
64	{at_workstation}
65	to coordinate and arrange
66	{st_container_s}
67	in sophisticated yet safe ways for me and my customer's work?
68	
69	{at_rbm_system} uses only `bash`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq` and a
70	{at_runtime}
71	(currently `docker`) natively to produce a safe and controlled space for development involving
72	{st_container_s}
73	heavily.
74	{at_rbm_system}
75	itself is largely a set of bash scripts designed to be easily incorporated into arbitrary projects via
76	`git subtree`, `git subrepo`, or `git submodule`
77	graft.
78	This is purely an open-source undertaking.
79	
80	=== Part One: {st_image} Management
81	
82	The
83	{at_rbm_system}
84	streamlines
85	{st_image}
86	creation and curation through Google Cloud Build (GCB) and Google Artifact Registry (GAR).
87	The
88	{at_build_service}:
89	
90	- Constructs
91	{st_image_s}
92	in clean, isolated environments using **Google-curated Cloud Build builder images**
93	- **Replicates trusted external images** into GAR with digest preservation and provenance tracking
94	- Supports multi-architecture builds via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation
95	- Generates software bills of material for compliance
96	- Captures and stores full build transcripts for provenance
97	- Maintains build transcripts, SBOMs, and commit references in an **auxiliary metadata artifact** separate from the runtime image
98	- Provides local trust posture inspection ({rbtgo_ark_inspect}) with compact and full views of build provenance, SLSA attestations, SBOM contents, and vouch verification results
99	- Stores validated
100	{st_image_s}
101	in Google Artifact Registry, tagged with minimal non-invasive labels
102	
103	This dual approach - building custom images and importing vetted external ones - ensures all container images used in development have known provenance and consistent tagging.
104	Small teams can maintain a curated registry combining custom-built tools with carefully selected upstream images, all traceable through GAR's unified storage.
105	
106	The system uses only
107	`curl`,
108	`jq`,
109	and
110	`bash`
111	scripts
112	to orchestrate both remote
113	{st_image}
114	construction and registry-to-registry replication, enabling small teams to maintain enterprise-grade
115	{st_image}
116	management practices without complex tooling.
117	`gcloud` is never required on the
118	{at_workstation};
119	it appears only inside Google-managed Cloud Build step containers (e.g. `gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud`) where it runs server-side during remote builds.
120	
121	Auxiliary provenance data, including the per-platform SBOM (`sbom.linux_amd64.spdx.json`), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries, is bundled into a compressed archive and uploaded to GAR as a **Generic Artifact** using the image tag as the package name and a fixed `metadata` version identifier.
122	This design keeps the primary image minimal and broadly usable while making rich build history available to those who need it.
123	
124	=== Part Two: {rbsc_crucible} Orchestration
125	
126	For development services requiring internet and/or IP connectivity at the
127	{at_workstation},
128	{at_rbm_system}
129	then orchestrates startup and configuration of
130	{rbsc_crucible_s}, which are comprised of a
131	{at_sentry_container},
132	a
133	{at_pentacle_container},
134	and a
135	{at_bottle_container}
136	operating together.
137	
138	The {at_rbm_system}
139	allows any
140	{st_image}
141	providing or using network services to function as a
142	{at_bottle_container}.
143	The
144	{at_pentacle_container}
145	establishes a privileged network namespace, configures it to route all traffic through the
146	{at_sentry_container},
147	then shares this pre-configured namespace with the
148	{at_bottle_container}.
149	This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the
150	{at_bottle_container}
151	experiences only a functional path to its
152	{at_sentry_container}
153	gateway.
154	
155	The
156	{at_sentry_container}
157	thus sets up a potentially sophisticated set of network security safeguards that prevent malicious or compromised
158	{at_bottle_container_s}
159	from exfiltrating the
160	{at_operator_p}
161	assets.
162	Through configuration of deeply mature tools `iptables` and `dnsmasq`, the
163	{at_sentry_container}
164	prevents such illegal accesses.
165	
166	By providing these controlled yet accessible tools, the
167	{at_rbm_system}
168	enables small development teams to maintain proper container hygiene throughout their workflow - from initial building through deployment and eventual cleanup.
169	This empowers organizations to leverage the expanding ecosystem of containerized development tools without requiring specialized DevOps expertise.
170	
171	== Project Direction
172	
173	The following themes represent future directions for Recipe Bottle, drawn from the project's horizon roadmap.
174	
175	**Build pipeline security hardening.**
176	Egress lockdown, VPC Service Controls, cosign image signing, and Binary Authorization are evaluated and consciously deferred.
177	Each has a defined trigger condition — they activate when compliance requirements, threat models, or deployment targets change.
178	Full analysis lives in the Cloud Build security posture documentation.
179	
180	**IAM identity infrastructure.**
181	Service account numeric IDs for faster propagation, universal read-back verification after IAM grants, and universal SA preflight checks.
182	These reduce retry frequency and strengthen grant correctness guarantees.
183	
184	**Container runtime expansion.**
185	Rootless deployment, macOS-native workflows, and alternative container runtimes beyond the current docker-on-Linux model.
186

192

1	= Getting Started with Recipe Bottle
2	
3	{at_recipe_bottle}
4	constructs container images under controlled conditions on trusted
5	{gcp_service}
6	infrastructure.
7	Developers retrieve these
8	{at_bottle_image_s}
9	and work within identical environments.
10	The
11	{rbtgi_repository}
12	serves as the single source of truth for container artifacts.
13	
14	This guide explains the setup sequence and ongoing operational patterns.
15	Most of what follows happens once; the final sections describe day-to-day work.
16	
17	== Core Concepts
18	
19	{at_recipe_bottle} contains workloads safely.
20	A _vessel_ defines what to build--a specification for a container workload.
21	An {rbtga_ark} is the built result: the immutable artifact produced from that vessel.
22	Multiple {rbtga_ark_s} can exist for a single vessel, each with its own build timestamp and provenance.
23	
24	Each {rbtga_ark} carries strong pedigree information including SBOMs and build metadata.
25	Organizations verify these attestations before deploying.
26	
27	== Depots and Roles
28	
29	A
30	{rbtgi_depot}
31	is the logical facility where container images are constructed and retained.
32	The
33	{rbtgi_repository}
34	within the {rbtgi_depot} stores all
35	{rbtgi_image_s}
36	available for retrieval.
37	Most organizations require exactly one {rbtgi_depot}.
38	
39	{at_recipe_bottle}
40	provides bash command-line administration of {rbtgi_depot} lifecycle and operations across roles.
41	
42	* *{rbtr_payor}*: Creates and funds {rbtgi_depot_s}; creates {rbtr_governor_s}. Billing flows through the {rbtr_payor_p} linked account. Manual OAuth setup enables all subsequent automation.
43	* *{rbtr_governor}*: Administers credentials within a {rbtgi_depot}. Creates {rbtr_director_s} and {rbtr_retriever_s} via service account authentication.
44	* *{rbtr_director}*: Submits builds, manages {rbtgi_image_s}.
45	* *{rbtr_retriever}*: Pulls {rbtgi_image_s} for local use.
46	
47	The
48	{rbtr_payor}
49	stands apart--requires manual console work and OAuth authentication for both {rbtgi_depot} and {rbtr_governor} lifecycle operations.
50	The
51	{rbtr_governor}
52	and all downstream roles authenticate via
53	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
54	credentials, enabling automation without human interaction.
55	
56	== Phase 1: Establish
57	
58	{rbtgo_payor_establish}
59	is a guide for manual steps taken using the Google Cloud Console to create the
60	{rbtgi_payor_project},
61	link a billing account,
62	enable required APIs,
63	and configure an OAuth 2.0 client.
64	
65	This phase produces a
66	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
67	containing OAuth client credentials.
68	This file must not be committed to version control.
69	
70	== Phase 2: Setup
71	
72	The administrator who completed establishment now executes automation from their
73	{at_workstation}.
74	
75	{rbtgo_payor_install}
76	takes the
77	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
78	and completes the OAuth handshake.
79	A browser window opens for authorization.
80	Upon success, the
81	{rbro_refresh_token}
82	is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions.
83	This file enables the
84	{rbtr_payor}
85	to authenticate for all future operations without repeating the browser flow.
86	
87	{rbtgo_depot_levy}
88	provisions the container build infrastructure.
89	Most installations require exactly one
90	{rbtgi_depot}-
91	a cohesive unit comprising a dedicated
92	{gcp_project_id},
93	a
94	{rbtgi_build_bucket},
95	a
96	{rbtgi_repository},
97	and the
98	{rbtr_mason}
99	service account that executes builds within the cloud.
100	
101	The
102	{rbtr_payor}
103	can manage multiple {rbtgi_depot_s}:
104	{rbtgo_depot_list}
105	displays all active {rbtgi_depot_s} and their status;
106	{rbtgo_depot_unmake}
107	permanently removes a {rbtgi_depot} and all its resources.
108	
109	The
110	{rbtr_payor}
111	then executes
112	{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
113	to create the
114	{rbtr_governor}
115	service account within the
116	{rbtgi_depot}.
117	This {rbtr_payor} operation produces an
118	{rbtgi_rbra_file},
119	stored at the path specified by
120	{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}.
121	The
122	{rbtr_governor}
123	can now operate independently of the
124	{rbtr_payor}.
125	
126	== Credential Administration
127	
128	The
129	{rbtr_governor}
130	manages the roster of credentialed users throughout the
131	{rbtgi_depot}
132	lifecycle.
133	While credential creation is typically infrequent, this administrative function remains active.
134	
135	{rbtgo_director_knight}
136	provisions a
137	{rbtr_director}
138	service account, producing an
139	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
140	at the path specified by
141	{rbrr_director_rbra_file}.
142	Directors submit builds and manage images.
143	
144	{rbtgo_retriever_charter}
145	provisions a
146	{rbtr_retriever}
147	service account, producing an
148	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
149	at the path specified by
150	{rbrr_retriever_rbra_file}.
151	Retrievers pull images for local use.
152	
153	The
154	{rbtr_governor}
155	also maintains visibility into issued credentials through
156	{rbtgo_sa_list}
157	and can revoke access through
158	{rbtgo_sa_forfeit}.
159	
160	== Credential Files
161	
162	Each {rbtr_role} maintains its own credential file.
163	Understanding these files helps administrators plan secure handling.
164	
165	The
166	{rbtr_payor}
167	credentials reside in `~/.rbw/rbro.env`, containing the
168	{rbro_client_secret}
169	and
170	{rbro_refresh_token}.
171	This file exists only on the administrator's
172	{at_workstation}.
173	
174	The
175	{rbtr_governor},
176	{rbtr_director},
177	and
178	{rbtr_retriever}
179	each have an
180	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
181	at paths defined in the repository configuration.
182	Each file contains
183	{giam_service_account}
184	credentials scoped to one role within one
185	{rbtgi_depot}.
186	
187	All credential files require `600` permissions, exclusion from version control, and secure distribution to authorized users.
188	A credential file cannot operate outside its designated role and {rbtgi_depot}.
189	
190	== Day-to-Day Operations
191	
192	Once setup and credential administration are complete, ongoing work proceeds through
193	{rbtr_director}
194	and
195	{rbtr_retriever}
196	roles.
197	
198	=== Director Operations
199	
200	The
201	{rbtr_director}
202	manages the build lifecycle.
203	
204	{rbtgo_ark_conjure}
205	submits a container build to
206	{gcb_service}
207	via `builds.create`.
208	The
209	{rbtr_mason}
210	executes the build within the cloud and publishes the resulting
211	{rbtgi_image}
212	to the
213	{rbtgi_repository}.
214	
215	{rbtgo_image_delete}
216	removes an
217	{rbtgi_image}
218	from the
219	{rbtgi_repository}.
220	
221	**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.
222	
223	=== Retriever Operations
224	
225	The
226	{rbtr_retriever}
227	accesses built artifacts.
228	
229	{rbtgo_image_retrieve}
230	pulls an
231	{rbtgi_image}
232	from the
233	{rbtgi_repository}
234	to the local
235	{at_workstation}.
236	
237	{rbtgo_ark_inspect}
238	displays the trust posture of a locally-present
239	{rbtga_ark},
240	reading build provenance, SBOM, and vouch results from the `-about` and `-vouch` artifacts retrieved by
241	{rbtgo_ark_summon}.
242	Use {rbtc_plumb_compact} for a summary or {rbtc_plumb_full} for complete detail including package inventory and Dockerfile.
243	
244	== Recovery
245	
246	If `~/.rbw/rbro.env` is lost, the administrator obtains a fresh
247	{rbtgi_json_key_file}
248	from Google Cloud Console and re-executes
249	{rbtgo_payor_install}.
250	
251	If OAuth tokens expire,
252	{rbtgo_payor_refresh}
253	obtains fresh credentials.
254	
255	If
256	{rbtr_governor}
257	credentials are compromised or need rotation, the
258	{rbtr_payor}
259	executes
260	{rbtgo_governor_mantle}
261	to replace the existing
262	{rbtr_governor}
263	service account with a fresh one, generating new
264	{rbtgi_rbra_file}
265	credentials.
266	
267	To revoke a user's access, the
268	{rbtr_governor}
269	executes
270	{rbtgo_sa_forfeit}
271	for the target service account, invalidating the corresponding
272	{rbtgi_rbra_file}.

1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.  Each item has a
5	rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference
6	(where the evidence lives).
7	
8	Items here are not planned for any heat.  When an item becomes actionable,
9	it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.  This is the single
10	collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should
11	cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
12	
13	===== IAM / Identity Infrastructure
14	
15	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
16	Google recommends using a {giam_service_account}'s numeric ID rather than its
17	email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce
18	propagation delay.  Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is
19	why propagation retry (<<rbscip_trade_study>> backoff profile) is necessary.
20	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require
21	threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains
22	that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.  Requires verification that
23	`setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
24	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in
25	depot creation or governor reset.
26	_Reference_: <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> (SA propagation section, evidence table).
27	
28	**Read-back verification universalization.**
29	Currently only {rbtoe_iam_grant_project} performs post-write verification
30	(re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
31	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo}, {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}, and {rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
32	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.  Adding verification universally would
33	provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout
34	window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production
36	and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
37	_Reference_: <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> (Convergence Analysis section).
38	
39	**SA preflight universalization.**
40	Currently only {rbtoe_iam_grant_sa} performs a pre-flight existence check on
41	the target {giam_service_account}.  Applying it to all grant types when the
42	member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior
43	for non-SA members.
44	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic
45	problem.
46	_Reference_: <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> (Convergence Analysis section).
47	
48	===== Container Runtime
49	
50	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
51	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle
52	lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).  Podman support is functional but not
53	hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement
54	assume Linux Podman with rootful access.  Expanding to rootless Podman,
55	Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes
56	would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
57	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows
58	beyond the current Podman VM approach.
59	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
60	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
61	
62	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
63	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks
64	pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before
65	returning.  `podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command
66	returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between
67	charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
68	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness
69	gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait
70	--condition=healthy`).
71	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
72	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
73	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
74	
75	**VM image supply chain targets GHCR, not GAR.**
76	The Podman VM image pipeline (`rbv_PodmanVM.sh`) mirrors machine-os disk
77	images from quay.io into GHCR as OCI containers, with per-platform tags,
78	brand-file provenance, and crane-based extraction inside an ignite VM.
79	This infrastructure has not been ported to GAR/Depot.  Podman deployment
80	requires either migrating the mirror target to GAR or maintaining a
81	parallel GHCR registry alongside the existing Depot project model.
82	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
83	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc; `rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (mirror,
84	fetch, init operations).
85	
86	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
87	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
88	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
89	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit
90	bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's
91	existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
92	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement
93	point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside
94	entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).  Nameplate model: two new
95	variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated
96	`RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).  No global
97	mode — peer access must always be explicit.  Implementation touches three
98	areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in
99	security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
100	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have
101	entry enabled.
102	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g.,
103	microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
104	_Reference_: Heat ₣AU paddock (design conversation capture).
105	
106	===== Network Security Model
107	
108	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
109	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports
110	to allowed IP ranges.  This is intentional: the spec defines
111	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist} as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR
112	ranges" — not port-restricted.  The DNS allowlist
113	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}) provides name-level precision; the CIDR
114	allowlist provides IP-level gating.  These two layers are independent and
115	reinforce each other.
116	+
117	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.  IANA's example.com
118	(RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from
119	`93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared
120	with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.  The DNS allowlist remains
121	precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination
122	within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.  A compromised
123	or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the
124	bottle.
125	+
126	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
127	+
128	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
129	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
130	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable
131	  collateral exposure.
132	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into
133	  iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses
134	  rather than static configuration.  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.  Requires
135	  new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
136	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and
137	  enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.  Closes the CDN gap at the
138	  application layer without dynamic iptables.  Requires a transparent TLS
139	  proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of
140	  complexity.
141	+
142	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the
143	threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
144	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules); .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env
145	(current CIDR configuration); ₣A1 heat ₢A1AAj (discovery context).
146	
147	===== Build Pipeline Security
148	
149	Items below are evaluated and consciously deferred.  Full analysis with
150	revisit triggers lives in RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc.
151	
152	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED (₣Av).**
153	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
154	Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
155	Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
156	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).
157	
158	**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
159	Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.  Requires
160	egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation,
161	and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
162	_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data
163	exfiltration risk assessment.
164	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
165	
166	**Cosign image signing.**
167	Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
168	KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
169	Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
170	_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside
171	GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
172	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).
173	
174	**Binary Authorization.**
175	Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
176	Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.  Not beneficial
177	until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
178	_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed
179	container platforms.
180	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).
181	
182	**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
183	Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in
184	vessel build contexts are not validated.  A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04`
185	uses a mutable tag.  Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
186	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
187	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).
188	
189	**Reproducibility verification.**
190	Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.  Requires reproducible
191	toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
192	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
193	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
